data.title: Market Signals: December Global Equity Brief
data.description:
Show me the money

Global equities closed November mixed, as investors began favoring proven earnings power over speculative growth. The MSCI World Index ended roughly flat for the month, with value, small-cap, and dividend-paying stocks outperforming large-cap growth names. Healthcare significantly outpaced information technology by over 12%.

Source: MSCI, Russell Investments

Fundamentals take the lead

After months dominated by AI-driven enthusiasm, November marked a subtle shift in tone. Investors appeared more discerning, rewarding firms that delivered measurable profit growth and penalizing those reliant on narrative. Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) strong quarterly results - underpinned by revenue momentum and expanding cloud computing business - stood out as evidence that solid execution still commands a premium.

In contrast, other high-profile names tied to the AI trade faced pressure. Companies making large capital expenditure announcements without corresponding revenue traction saw muted or negative reactions. This divide suggests markets may be entering a new phase in which execution and profitability matter more than potential.

We believe this re-emphasis on fundamentals is healthy. After a long stretch of sentiment-driven performance, the pendulum appears to be swinging back toward earnings quality and balance-sheet strength - conditions that typically favor active, selective management.

AI enthusiasm meets reality

Could the AI bubble be deflating? While enthusiasm for transformative technologies remains high, investors are asking harder questions about return on investment. Market reaction to recent announcements in semiconductors, cloud infrastructure, and enterprise software indicates that capital spending alone is no longer enough to sustain momentum.

We see this as an early sign of normalization rather than a breakdown. The AI theme remains intact but is evolving from early-stage optimism to demonstrating real-world results.

Over time, this shift could help distinguish established leaders from players with less-proven business models. For long-term investors, the key may be identifying businesses that can translate technological leadership into scalable earnings and sustainable cash flow.

Macro backdrop: Slowing but stable

Beyond the AI narrative, global macro conditions continue to moderate. Leading indicators point to softer labor markets, slower demand growth, and sticky inflation. Central banks appear cautious but not overly restrictive, balancing the need to manage inflation expectations with the desire to avoid triggering a downturn.

These cross-currents are shaping a more discriminating market environment. We believe higher-quality companies - with resilient business models and steady cash generation - are likely to stand out in this phase. In our view, diversification across sectors, styles, and geographies remains essential as equity leadership broadens beyond the narrow band of mega-cap technology.

Investor implications

Equity markets are shifting from story-driven rallies to results-driven scrutiny. We believe investors can benefit from portfolios that combine selective exposure to innovation with broader diversification and a quality bias.

The ability to balance participation in long-term growth themes - such as AI - with protection against volatility may define success in the year ahead. In our view, fundamentals and discipline will be critical as markets enter 2026.

Important information pertaining to the hypothetical example: Past performance does not predict future returns. Return level is proportionately scaled in line with cash level to be overlaid. Source: Russell Investments. Assumptions: Average cash level 1.0%, 10-year history from 12/31/2023, gross of fees. Opportunity cost from not securitizing cash varies by asset allocation and time period, and is represented by horizontal bars as marked within the chart legend. Target asset allocation used: 0% cash, 74% MSCI World, 26% Global Aggregate (GBP Hedged). For illustrative purposes only. Does not represent any actual investment. Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be invested in directly. Performance benefit (NET) of overlaying cash by last 5 individual calendar year is as follows: 2023:20 bps, 2022:-17bps, 2021:16bps, 2020:14bps, 2019:23bps.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

data.title: WTI Rangebound: How Stalled Peace Talks And OPEC Strategy Will Shape The Oil Market
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Oil prices climbed on Thursday, with WTI oil ending the day with an increase of roughly 1%, trading at $59.70 a barrel.

The upside move was primarily fueled by renewed geopolitical risk. Specifically, news of Ukrainian strikes on Russian oil infrastructure and the backdrop of stalled peace negotiations created supply disruption fears, encouraging traders to push prices marginally higher.

Ukraine-Russia Peace Talks

Oil prices received support from the fact that peace talks for Ukraine seemed to be stalling, especially after representatives for President Trump met with the Kremlin but reported no major progress toward ending the war. Previously, hopes for a peace deal had kept prices lower, as traders worried that Russian oil would flood an already full global market.

In related news, Ukraine has continued to use drones to attack Russian oil infrastructure. A recent example was the fifth attack on the Druzhba pipeline, which sends Russian oil to Hungary and Slovakia, though the pipeline operator later stated that supplies were flowing normally.

Experts say that Ukraine’s drone campaign against Russian refineries has become more organized and effective. This campaign has caused Russian refining output to drop by about 335,000 barrels per day between September and November compared to last year, with the production of gasoline and gasoil being the most severely affected.

The geopolitical risks appear to be balancing out other factors such as high oil stockpiles and OPEC's production strategy. These factors are likely to keep any major moves at bay for now.

Forward Outlook - Bulls or Bears to Prevail?

Oil prices appear destined to remain range-bound for the foreseeable future. Markets will continue to wait for a major catalyst in the form of some peace between Russia-Ukraine, or a significant change in OPEC+ strategy.

Neither situation seems likely to change anytime soon while clouds continue to hang over the global economic landscape.

Overall, the downside does appear to be favored given that supply/demand concerns appear to be structural at this stage.

With that in mind, traders should be cautious about any time oil prices go up. Until there is clear proof that producers are cutting back supply, or that global demand has increased enough to use up the extra oil currently available, these price increases are likely to be temporary and will soon be followed by renewed selling.

Technical Analysis - WTI

From a technical analysis standpoint, WTI continues to struggle with significant sideways movement.

A descending trendline break has taken place, but given the overarching macro picture, any move at this stage may meet significant selling pressure.

The period-14 RSI has crossed above the neutral 50 level, which does hint at bullish momentum.

Immediate upside resistance rests at 60.00 before the 61.67-62.12 zone comes into focus. Beyond that, we have the 200-day MA which rests at 63.66.

Support to the downside rests at 58.00 before the November 25 swing low at 57.00 comes into focus.

WTI Crude Oil Daily Chart, November 4, 2025

(Source: TradingView)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Beyond Mega-Cap Mania: Rebalancing Toward Small-Mid Caps For Diversified Equity Exposure
data.description:
The stock market of the past few years has revolved around a familiar theme: a handful of mega-cap giants driving most of the index-level performance. Their influence has become so large that broad-market exposure is not as balanced as it appears.

Concentration has always played a role in market cycles, yet the current level is striking. When so much of an index’s behavior depends on a few companies, many clustered in the same industry, investors should ask whether their portfolios still reflect genuine diversification.

Market history offers perspective. Periods like the late 1960s and the dot-com boom saw similar waves of investor enthusiasm for dominant companies. Leadership looked unshakable in the moment, yet many of the top firms ultimately lagged once conditions shifted.

Even in 2000, when the ten largest companies made up a far smaller share of the S&P 500 than today, only one of those leaders went on to outperform the broader market in the decades that followed.¹ Concentration can mask fragility, especially when investor expectations inflate faster than underlying fundamentals.

Why Small and Mid Caps Matter Again

That context makes the case for revisiting small and mid-cap exposure. After years of lagging, smaller companies now trade at some of the most attractive relative valuations seen in decades.

Research shows that small caps recently priced at a steep discount to large caps, a level that historically has preceded stronger relative returns.² Markets routinely overreact to near-term headwinds, and the gap between perception and reality can widen to a point where opportunities emerge for long-term investors.

Small and mid-cap stocks also provide characteristics that complement rather than mimic the mega-cap profile. Over extended periods, smaller companies have delivered higher average returns, thanks in part to their capacity for faster growth from a smaller base.³

Their performance tends to be more cyclical, which has worked against them during periods of economic uncertainty or tight financial conditions. Yet this same sensitivity can become an advantage when recoveries begin, as smaller firms often rebound with greater force once conditions brighten.²

Interest rate dynamics matter as well. Many small-cap firms rely more heavily on costlier financing, making them more vulnerable during tightening cycles. As policy eventually eases and borrowing costs decline, these pressures can reverse quickly.

Analysts note that falling rates often improve both credit access and profitability for smaller companies, creating a tailwind that large caps may not experience to the same degree.²

The Case for Rebalancing

None of this diminishes the value of owning established leaders. Mega-caps remain essential components of many portfolios. The argument is simply that balance matters. Small and mid-cap stocks tap into parts of the economy underrepresented in an index dominated by large technology firms: industrials, regional financials, energy infrastructure, and domestic consumer businesses. Their different drivers can help smooth portfolio behavior as leadership rotates.

If the market broadens, investors positioned only in the giants may miss the next leg of opportunity. Reintroducing smaller companies into the mix is not a contrarian bet; it is a recognition that no cycle persists indefinitely. Diversification ensures exposure to what is working today and what may lead tomorrow.

Footnotes

  1. Justin McNichols, “The S&P 500 Concentration,” Osborne Partners, January 16, 2025.

  2. Daniel Fang, “Concerned About Market Concentration and Lofty Valuations? Consider Small Caps,” CFA Institute Enterprising Investor, June 19, 2024.

  3. Dáire Dunne and Irmak Surenkok, “A Turning Point for US Small Caps,” Wellington Management, 2023.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. data.title: EM High Yield Corporates: Superior Yields, Low Defaults
data.description:
EM high yield has extended its 2025 momentum, delivering strong carry and compelling yields. With higher credit quality and lower defaults, the segment offers a more attractive risk profile than US high yield.

Emerging market high yield corporate bonds continued their momentum in 2025, outpacing US and major global credit benchmarks after a robust gain of approximately 13% in 2024. Over the past 15 years, this EM debt segment has more than doubled in size, evolving into a broader and deeper market. Today, the asset class benefits from improving fundamentals, higher credit quality and lower default rates than US high yield, while offering a substantial yield cushion.

Mind the Yield

EM high yield corporates continue to offer some of the most attractive yields in the fixed income space. The income portion of the return has been the dominant driver of performance, providing steady carry that cushions volatility and smooths drawdowns through market swings. This dynamic, where carry leads the way over larger price gains, can deliver consistent returns without relying on further spread tightening.

Attractive Compensation for Risk

EM high yield corporate bonds are often perceived as riskier than those in the US and other developed markets, yet they exhibit higher credit quality, lower defaults and superior compensation for risk. The EM high yield index tilts more toward higher quality, with approximately 62% rated BB versus 55% in US high yield. Default rates spiked post-COVID in 2022-2023 following China’s property sector collapse and Russian sanctions but have since normalized to levels below US high yield. Further, EM high yield corporate issuers often display wider spreads and lower leverage than US high yield, offering a relatively more favorable risk/reward profile.

Lower Leverage than US HY

The EM high yield corporate debt market has matured into a larger, more diversified market that presents an attractive opportunity for income investors: higher yields and improved credit quality relative to US high yield.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

USD EM Bonds are represented by the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index. EM High Yield is represented by the BofA Diversified High Yield US Emerging Markets Corporate Plus Index. US High Yield is represented by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index. Local Currency EM Sovereign is represented by the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Index. US IG is represented by the ICA BofA US Corporate Index. US Broad Market is represented by the ICE BofA US Broad Index. Global Broad Market is represented by the ICE BofA Global Broad Index. Developed Markets is represented by the ICE BofA Developed Markets Sovereign Index.

There are inherent risks with fixed income investing. These risks may include interest rate, call, credit, market, inflation, government policy, liquidity, or junk bond. When interest rates rise, bond prices fall. This risk is heightened with investments in longer duration fixed-income securities and during periods when prevailing interest rates are low or negative.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: UWM Holdings Pays You To Wait For A Recovery In The Housing Market
data.description:
I have written about companies that have been impacted by the sluggish real estate market in the US for most of this year. I have written about the private mortgage insurers like Enact Holdings (ACT), Radian Group (RDN), and NMI Holdings (NMIH). I have also written about a number of mortgage providers and community banks that are heavily dependent on home mortgages for their future growth. In most cases, it was hard for me to see a reasonable risk-reward equation that made those companies worthy of a Buy rating. My first noticeable exception was Northpointe Bancshares (NPB). While it is far too early to do a victory lap, NPB has gained just over 5% since that article was published as the broader market has been flat.

My pessimism for all of these companies was not always founded on the companies themselves but rather on the current state of the home-buying market in the US that has been providing hurricane-strength headwinds to their entire industry. I think that 2026 could finally be the year that things start to "normalize" in the sense that there is a better balance between buyers and sellers, and interest rates are low enough to sustain an uptick in both buying activity and refinancing. Some of the companies that have been stuck in the mud for the last 12 to 18 months may finally start to claw their way out. One of those companies that has potential for meaningful gains in 2026 is UWM Holdings Corporation (UWMC).

The company is the top mortgage lender in the US and the largest wholesale lender based on data from 2024. Its total amount for all loans - purchase, refinance, and home improvement - was over $40 billion larger than its closest competitor, Rocket Mortgage (RKT). Yet, UWM Holdings is far from a household name, and its stock has been languishing for an extended period of time. Over the last 12 months, the share price for UWM has fallen 12.5%. Over the last five years, the decline is even more pronounced at 43.8%.

A higher mortgage rate environment has plagued the industry for several years, but signs of a turnaround are starting to peek through the clouds. As part of its last earnings call in November, UWM President and CEO Mathew Ishbia stated that the company closed $41.7 billion of production, marking its best quarter since 2021. At that time, mortgage rates were in the 2.5% to 3% range.

The mortgage business is not totally out of the woods yet. It may take several years for things to behave as they did pre-pandemic. The recovery will not be consistently smooth, and household debt in other areas - credit cards, student loans, car loans, etc. - is adding pressure on consumers in the US. Nonetheless, I think UWM deserves a Buy rating based on the data I will discuss here. While I think there is certainly more risk here than I normally accept with some of my other investments, I also think there is a great opportunity for above average gains.

Company Overview

With its headquarters in Pontiac, Michigan, UWM Holdings Corporation is a member of the commercial and residential mortgage finance sector. The company is involved in almost all aspects of the mortgage process, from origination to servicing loans over the long term. UWM offers Conventional Elite, FHA, VA, and USDA home loans. The company has a current market cap of $9.4 billion.

According to the company's Q3 2025 10-Q, UWM controls just over $17.0 billion in assets, an increase of 8.6% from the end of 2024. The fair value of its mortgage loans increased 13.3% during that same time period, reaching almost $10.8 billion by the end of the most recent quarter. Liabilities are climbing steadily for the company as well. Stockholders' equity has actually fallen since the start of the year from about $2.1 billion to $1.6 billion.

There is no doubt that the company has a dominant position in the mortgage industry, and the most recent quarter is a sign of the company's ability to continue to produce a high level of activity even as the housing market in the US flounders. September provided an all-time record for the company as it secured $4.8 billion of mortgage rate locks in a single day. After closing almost $42 billion in loans in Q3, UWM projects Q4, a normally slower time of the year for home buying, to produce between $43 billion and $50 billion.

UWM benefits from a strong customer experience, which can be seen through its net promoter score of 88.7 in Q3 2025. This places the company in a category that is considered "world class" and is significantly better than the average company in the finance sector. Factors that lead to such a high score in that industry include trust, high levels of customer service, and high-quality company infrastructure.

One of the more attractive aspects of an investment in UWMC is its dividend yield. The company currently provides shareholders with a quarterly payout of $0.10. This gives the stock an annual yield of 7.1% at its current price. The dividend rate for the company has been consistent since the first quarter of 2021, and given the recent earnings level from the company, that rate is unlikely to increase in the near future. However, a decrease in the dividend amount could dramatically impact investor sentiment since the yield may be what has been keeping investors involved with the company during the last few years of declining stock prices.

Analysis of UWM Holdings

Stocks do not perform like UWMC has in the last five years without a good reason. Despite all of the positives mentioned above, the company has struggled to see consistent benefits on its bottom line. UWM Holdings has also established a recent pattern of disappointing analysts' earnings expectations.

Non-GAAP earnings of just $0.01 per share in Q3 2025 were $0.06 below expectations. This marked the fifth quarter out of the last six when the company has failed to reach the bar set by Wall Street ahead of its financial reporting. The lone bright spot for UWMC is the $0.16 of profit reported in Q2 2025. This was the highest recorded on a per share basis for the company since Q3 2022. Higher loan production, higher margins, and greater operational efficiency were all factors in the results from Q2.

Even with this strong second quarter, UWMC trades at a PE of almost 38. Other mortgage providers trade at much lower multiples. PennyMac (PFSI) has a PE of just 11. Northpointe Bancshares, which also has some traditional banking operations, trades at a PE of 8.15. Onity Group (ONIT) trades at a PE of 4.5 with a market cap of just $352 million. ONIT also has some operations overseas, so the comparisons are not exact, but given these other numbers, UWMC certainly does not appear to be a bargain.

The average EPS estimate for the company in FY 2025 is $0.15. It has already earned $0.19 in the first nine months of this year, so it appears likely to exceed that mark. Next year, the company is projected to earn $0.45 per share, which would put its PE around 12.5. The problem, of course, is the company's recent history of missing these expectations. So what exactly gives me reason for optimism?

A big part of my thesis is based on how well UWM Holdings has been able to weather these last three years. As the graph below shows, since their high in 2021, existing home sales in the US have been on a steep decline. In spite of this, UWM has been able to keep its loan origination numbers at a consistent level, taking share away from the competition. Should home sales increase in 2026, the company appears poised to make significant gains.

Another positive for the company is its recent investments in technology that should give it an added boost in the years ahead. In May of this year, the company rolled out "Mia", a generative AI loan officer assistant. Since its implementation, Mia has made over 400,000 calls with borrowers, and roughly 14,000 have led to closings. This success rate is higher than even UWMC expected.

Also in the second quarter of this year, UWM Holdings launched "LEO" which it called "an innovative tool that provides a detailed analysis of competitors' Loan Estimates (LE) and identifies gaps and opportunities for better deals for the borrower." LEO gives independent mortgage brokers the ability to offer customers higher quality loan estimates and, therefore, win over more clients. The tool allows brokers to upload loan estimates into another company tool, UWM Chat, so that they can be analyzed at a faster speed. A complete list of the company's AI tools can be seen in the chart below.

Earlier this year, UWM insiders showed a great deal of optimism for the company's future, as there were six different insider buys in March. Five of the six transactions had a total value of over $25,000. One, from the company CEO, involved over 108,000 shares and $680,000. UWMC currently trades at just over $0.50 lower than the price for those purchases.

Risks to Consider

There are some significant risks to consider when making an investment in UWM Holdings. The company currently has a BB- debt rating from Fitch, a score that places the company's obligations below investment grade. According to the rating commentary, the company is impacted by the "highly cyclical nature of the mortgage origination business and a reliance on secured, short-term, uncommitted funding facilities". Fitch notes that UWM faces higher "key person risk" due to the significant control that the CEO and his family have over the company as majority shareholders. Another concern mentioned was the company's exclusive focus on the wholesale channel that "limits potential market share gains in the overall mortgage market". While a downgrade from Fitch is unlikely in the short term, UWM's current rating places it just one notch above a rating that would be considered "highly speculative".

There are no guarantees that the housing market in the US will be stronger in 2026 than it was this year. In the last couple of months, there have been a wide range of opinions from experts on the health of the current market. While CNBC reported that it may be in a "rare state of balance" in September, Newsweek reported that the US is experiencing a "deflationary vortex" for home prices in October. For the pace of home buying to pick up, consumers need home prices to be more affordable. For mortgage refinance activity to increase significantly for an extended period of time, mortgage rates need to fall, likely to a level around or just below 6%.

The Bottom Line

The mortgage industry in the US flourished in the ultra-low interest rate environment that followed the COVID-19 pandemic. The troubles seen in the market today are really part of a hangover from the party-like atmosphere from just a few years ago. Because the boom cycle that followed the pandemic with rates below 3% was likely a once-in-a-lifetime event, the recovery is also taking on several unique characteristics. There is a strong chance that 2026 will not be that much better than 2025. If the market does gain the ability to operate in a more normal manner with stable to slightly declining interest rates, it may have to contend with another headwind: recessionary pressures from the overall economy.

For investors who have a longer-term investment outlook, a small position in UMWC could pay off handsomely if the stars align next year. If not, those same investors should be able to collect the high dividend yield as payment for their patience. Given the company's current debt situation, I am not sure that maintaining the high yield for the company is in its best interest, but the effect of cutting the dividend could be even more damaging to investor sentiment.

The company's market position, investments in technology, and recent performance are enough for me to consider its stock to be a speculative Buy.

data.title: How Tariffs And Technology Reshaped The U.S. Economy In 2025 And What Comes Next
data.description:
Macro Signposts highlights takeaways from the data analysis conducted by our team of economists and other experts.

The U.S. economy has been surprisingly resilient in 2025. Growth held up well as policy-related pressures had offsetting impacts: Many companies accelerated artificial intelligence (AI) deployment and investment while reassessing or consolidating labor forces, mainly in an effort to manage tariff-related costs.

In 2026, a reasonable baseline outlook is that the U.S. economy will get fresh support from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act’s front-loaded fiscal easing, which should help to broaden growth and stabilize the labor market. Nevertheless, there are risks.

Tariff, tax, and trade policies, along with a new technology investment wave, are creating winners and losers across the economy. The push and pull between these groups and the potential fits and starts in investment cycles could have implications either way. Shifting personnel at the Federal Reserve creates additional uncertainty.

Before we discuss these forward-looking issues, it’s important to think about how things have progressed in 2025 and where the surprises have been.

The story of 2025: Tariff and tax policy accelerated AI deployment

U.S. real GDP growth was surprisingly resilient in 2025 amid the Trump administration’s fiscal, trade, and immigration policy changes. We await further economic data releases (delayed by the government shutdown), and for now we estimate real U.S. GDP grew between 1.5–2.0% in 2025.

That’s down from the 2.5–3.0% pace in 2024, according to the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA), but much better than many expected. Similarly, core U.S. inflation remained elevated at 3% (according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS)), but was also more benign than many had feared, as tariff-related adjustments were more gradual and uneven across industries.

Benign growth and inflation didn’t mean there was an absence of policy adjustments. Rather, policy actions – and their interaction with new technologies – drove unexpected macro outcomes: Tax and tariff policies accelerated AI deployment, investment, and wealth creation, with offsetting implications for growth and inflation (see Figure 1).

Figure 1: Estimated contributions to U.S. GDP (trailing 4 quarters) of various factors vs. 2% trend

Source: Haver Analytics, Bloomberg, PIMCO calculations as of November 2025. September and December data are estimates based on private sector reports.

Indeed, a more detailed look at 2025 U.S. economic performance reveals stark divergences under the surface. Large, capital-intensive companies with more limited exposure to tariff costs, or companies able to offset those costs with more generous investment tax credits, were relative winners.

Small and midsize labor-intensive companies exposed to the trade sectors or immigration policy changes were relative losers. Credit and equity markets have repriced credit risks in these sectors. The existence of winners and losers within and across industries had three important macro implications.

First, elevated competition limited overall corporate pricing power and tempered tariff-related consumer price adjustments. We estimate around 40% to 50% of the tariff costs were passed onto consumers, lifting core inflation by around 0.4 to 0.5 percentage points (ppts).

Within industries, larger, more capital-intensive companies that could capture offsetting tax cuts were better positioned to compete on pricing and absorb tariff costs. This forced a broader focus on defending margins through cost savings initiatives – especially labor cost savings.

Second, the search for ways to cut costs likely accelerated AI deployment, which in turn contributed to the 2025 pickup in hyperscalers’ capital outlays, plus large investment announcements for 2026 and beyond. We estimate AI and related investment, and the wealth it generated, contributed roughly 0.5 ppts to 2025 real U.S. GDP growth.

Data center investment, including structures, servers, chips, and other components, grew $200 billion nominally in 2025, although the contribution to real GDP growth was limited due to reliance on imported servers. Software and R&D investment also accelerated and were a larger contribution to GDP, while AI-driven stock market returns also contributed to surprisingly resilient consumption from wealth creation despite slower real labor income growth.

Third, labor market challenges offset some of the technology-driven momentum by reducing real household labor income growth below 1%, according to our estimates. Policy shifts directly or indirectly fueled the labor market challenges: Tariff adjustments weighed on labor demand, and immigration policy changes reduced labor supply.

We estimate changing immigration policy reduced the breakeven rate of monthly payroll growth needed to stabilize unemployment from an estimated peak rate of roughly 200,000 per month in 2023 and 2024 down to 50,000 per month in 2025.

Similarly, reported payroll growth decelerated from a roughly 110,000 monthly pace in 2024 to a 50,000 average monthly pace in 2025. (All employment data are from the BLS.) However, the unemployment rate rose 0.4 ppts to 4.4% thus far in 2025, suggesting labor demand fell faster than labor supply.

The ratio of job openings to unemployed also fell, with professional and business services, retail, and construction categories – industries most exposed to tariffs and tech themes – declining the most.

In 2026: resilient growth, sticky inflation, stabilizing labor markets

The combination of policy and macro factors supported more resilient, yet more narrowly focused U.S. growth in 2025. While we expect the resilience to continue in 2026, there are also good reasons to expect a broader demand recovery under the surface, which could stabilize (and even tighten) the labor market, further delaying progress on bringing inflation to target.

Net new fiscal stimulus from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act will likely provide fresh support to after-tax incomes of households and businesses, helping offset the tariff-related drag. Business investment tax incentives were retroactively applied and will result in a one-time cash injection for corporates able to take advantage of up-front capital expense credits.

Households will also receive larger refund checks in early 2026 due to the lower state and local tax credit cap, expanded child tax credits and remitted taxes paid on 2025 overtime and tips income. We estimate new household tax relief could amount to $200 billion next year – that’s about 0.6 ppts of GDP – with roughly half of that coming via refunds.

The AI/tech investment cycle should also continue to support the economy. Greater spending on AI implementation, software, and research and development across a broader range of companies could help offset some cooling in data-center-related capex.

Trade policy uncertainty and tariff-related economic drag should also diminish in 2026 (see Figure 2). The Supreme Court is likely to strike down the Trump administration’s IEEPA tariffs (those implemented under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act), which would force the Trump administration to refund roughly $100 billion of tariffs paid in 2025 and pivot to a national security framework reliant on Section 232 product tariffs and Section 301 trading partner tariffs.

As tariff policy becomes more certain and business adjustments run their course, investment and hiring should reaccelerate somewhat. Private sector U.S. investment commitments announced with the Trump administration are an additional possible tailwind.

Figure 2: Estimated quarterly impact of tariff, tax, and spending policy changes (positive numbers are supportive of growth)

More Info

Source: U.S. Joint Committee on Taxation, U.S. Congressional Budget Office, U.S. Treasury, PIMCO calculations as of November 2025

Reasonable baselines don’t come without risks

While we see a reasonable baseline that broadening demand is likely to maintain the U.S.’s surprising economic resilience, that outlook doesn’t come without key questions and key risks. For example:

  • Will the AI investment cycle continue to provide the wealth and growth impulse it did in 2025?
  • Will U.S. investment broaden as uncertainty fades?
  • Will the companies that aren’t well-positioned for the new policy environment contribute to a broader default cycle, with negative implications for labor markets?
  • Will higher-income households continue to drive resilient consumption?
  • How will changes in Fed personnel change how the Federal Open Market Committee views the balance of risks and therefore interest rate policy?

Bottom line

The economic adjustments driven by tariffs, taxes, and technological innovation surprised many in 2025, and the U.S. economy will likely surprise forecasters again in 2026.

At PIMCO, a focus on the baseline outlook is just the start. We believe it’s an understanding of the risks that will generate potential performance for our clients.

Disclosures

All investments contain risk and may lose value.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Investors should consult their investment professional prior to making an investment decision. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice

Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. data.title: How Private And Public Markets Are Combining To Fund The AI Buildout
data.description:
By Henna Nordqvist, CFA | Sydney Kyne | William Smith

We see benefits in sharing funding needs across public and private markets.

Increasing numbers of investment-grade corporate borrowers are tapping private credit to finance major infrastructure projects. We think there are clear benefits to sharing funding needs across public and private markets. Here’s why.

Growth Is Happening Everywhere

Private credit isn’t what it used to be. Once a niche corner of finance focused on direct lending to high-risk, private equity–owned companies, it’s since blossomed into a thriving, diverse market where billions also flow into asset-backed loans and bespoke investment-grade deals for large corporations.

The growth has been driven in part by borrowers that need bespoke financing arrangements, often for vast infrastructure investment. Demand is also robust among a wide array of market participants, from retail investors to insurance companies eager for quality assets tailored to their liabilities.

The result is a market that—while less liquid than public markets—is beginning to rival them in depth and breadth. Thus, companies today navigate an increasingly efficient range of funding options that allows them to choose between public and private lenders. Many choose both.

Balancing Out the AI Investment Cycle

That makes sense. Flexibility in funding solutions is especially valuable for capex-intensive sectors and those involved in them, such as the hyperscalers that are rapidly building out artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. These firms—primarily Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet Inc., Meta Platforms and Oracle—have an insatiable need for financing. S&P estimates that spending on global AI information technology will grow between 28% and 36% annually over the next five years.

When it comes to data centers and the energy sources needed to power the growth of AI, we believe in an “all-of-the-above” approach to capital formation. In our view, it reinforces a more sustainable and balanced AI investment cycle.

Earlier this year, Meta Platforms (META) raised nearly $60 billion to support its data center build-out through two major transactions. The first, issued by a joint venture between Meta's affiliate and a private lender, was tailored to support the project’s construction. Although executed as a private placement, the notes are tradable in the public investment-grade market. Meta then supplemented this financing with a $30 billion public investment-grade bond issuance, rated Aa3/AA–, further broadening its funding base.

In coming years, these hyperscalers are expected to issue significant amounts of debt to fund two core workloads: large-scale training superclusters, which require heavy upfront capex, and globally distributed inference capacity, which drives more recurring infrastructure investment. These companies generally have healthy cash flows and strong balance sheets that make it possible to fund major AI infrastructure projects with both public and private debt at a massive scale. Most have the ability to absorb significant incremental leverage without causing imminent credit downgrades, in our analysis.

In public credit markets, liquidity depends on the ability of investors to buy and sell bonds easily. This is only possible when there’s a steady pipeline of new and existing bonds from a diverse set of issuers. The scale, speed and duration of public issuance have shown that the market remains active, liquid and resilient.

There are other potential benefits as well. Hyperscaler credit spreads have already moved wider as investors react to the sheer volume of expected supply, balance sheet growth and a longer-dated payback timeline. This presents the opportunity to lock in durable income with issuers whose balance sheets can weather a more volatile AI-adoption path.

Private, Public Credit Serve Different Needs

Private credit will likely continue to provide some types of financing that public markets can’t—or won’t—provide. That may include lending against more complex, less liquid assets. While some supply disruptions are possible, we think this kind of demand poses little threat to public investment-grade bonds available to both institutions and retail investors.

Moreover, traditional middle-market direct corporate lending remains a sizable part of the private credit market. This segment is still populated by companies with sub-investment-grade ratings that may not have access to more traditional sources of credit or may require bespoke lending solutions that private lenders are best equipped to provide. That leaves sufficient investment-grade supply for traditional investors.

Public Credit Faces Risk but Finds Resilience

The expansion of private credit introduces some focused risks for public bond investors. When public issuers tap private markets without comparable disclosure, off-balance sheet obligations can be harder to model, and newer private credit structures may add complexity. Lower transparency, on its own, is a headwind for public-market analysis, and it can also weigh on credit ratings.

But in our view, systemic concerns are overstated. The major borrowers—particularly the hyperscalers—remain high-quality credits, while private credit lenders still have ample dry powder, and public investment-grade fundamentals are solid. We believe diversified funding sources can help distribute both the cost and risk of building data centers in this ongoing AI infrastructure cycle.

It’s possible that expanded demand for private credit may at times pressure investment-grade supply at the margins. But from our vantage point, the migration of capital to private markets doesn’t pose a significant risk to the public investment-grade one. Instead, it reflects diversification of funding sources and investment opportunities.

Ultimately, the steady supply of public investment-grade bonds—driven by ongoing issuance from major borrowers—remains a cornerstone of public-market liquidity, ensuring that investors can transact efficiently even as private credit expands.

The public investment-grade market continues to serve a broad range of borrowers and investors. We expect it to remain resilient even as the broader credit landscape evolves.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: abrdn Australia Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund returned 2.25% (in net asset value terms) and underperformed its benchmark over the review period. This was due to the overweight to healthcare and weak stock selection in information technology, consumer discretionary and real estate during a volatile August reporting period. The weakness was partially offset by the positive contributions from our financials and industrials holdings.[1]

Through the quarter, the healthcare sector lagged as investors rotated from defensive high-quality sectors into cyclical sectors, such as materials and financials, benefiting from commodity strength and rate expectations. A hawkish RBA stance following strong inflation data further weighed on sentiment, as rising rate expectations hurt premium valuations. As a result, the Fund’s heavier-than-benchmark exposure to this sector detracted from performance. Among our holdings, CSL (OTCQX:CMXHF) shares slumped primarily on the back of mixed FY25 results, with revenue growth slowing year-on-year, below market expectations. Margins were pressured by weaker vaccine performance and this, along with cautious guidance, led to a sharp market reaction. Other detractors included Pro Medicus, which had rallied in prior months, and Resmed, given the broader sector weakness.

Across other sectors, in IT, Xero’s (OTCPK:XROLF) share price weakness reflected the soft sentiment towards the software sector amid concerns about AI disruption and pricing power, while subdued UK subscriber additions added to near-term uncertainty despite revenue staying on track. As for the consumer discretionary sector, Aristocrat Leisure faced pressure from softening US gaming trends and cautious investor sentiment, despite strong fundamentals in gaming and margin upside potential. In real estate, more hawkish interest rate expectations saw general weakness across the real estate sector.

The losses were mitigated by strength in other sectors. In financials, NAB (OTCPK:NABZY) and ANZ (OTCPK:ANZGY) performed well as investors rotated into banks amid a stabilising rate environment and resilient credit quality. Both institutions delivered solid earnings updates, highlighting disciplined cost control and capital management. Outside of banks, Hub24 also performed well, as it continued to capture market share in wealth management through its scalable, in-house technology platform. Strong execution drove margin expansion and operating leverage, supported by rising fund flows and adviser adoption.

Among our industrials holdings, ALS (OTCPK:CPBLF) stood out as the company, which offers lab testing for mining, oil and gas, environmental, food, and pharmaceuticals, continued to benefit from structural growth trends in testing services. Regulatory momentum around PFAS testing and a recovery in mineral exploration spending boosted demand across its diversified portfolio.

Elsewhere in materials, Northern Star Resources (OTCPK:NESRF) was the top stock contributor to performance after gold prices rose on the back of geopolitical uncertainty and central bank buying. Its disciplined capital allocation and strong balance sheet reinforced investor confidence, while its position as a laggard in the gold space attracted incremental flows. Another positive contributor was Capstone Copper Corp (OTCPK:CSCCF), on the back of further positive sentiment around copper.

Market review

Australian equities advanced in the October quarter, with the benchmark ASX 200 Index (AS51) returning 4.37% in US dollar terms. The index hit a new all-time high during the quarter although the strength was primarily attributable to a shift away from year-to-date strong performers into lower quality laggards and thematics. During the quarter, the financials and materials sectors outperformed at the expense of the healthcare sector. In financials, we saw weakness in Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CBA) and strength in the three other major Australian banks ANZ, Westpac (OTCPK:WEBNF), and National Australia Bank (NAB). Elsewhere, while the materials sector performed well, there was notable strength in laggards such as Mineral Resources and Fortescue, rare earth refiner Lynas, and lithium companies, such as Pilbara Minerals and Liontown Resources.

The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) had to mull over two key data points during October, which provided insight into its two biggest concerns: inflation and the labour market. Of those, unemployment came in much higher than expected, at 4.5% for September. Conversely, the RBA seemed more concerned with the higher-than-expected quarterly inflation figures. Headline inflation accelerated to 1.3% quarter on quarter (q/q) and 3.2% year on year (y/y), which was the largest increase since early 2023. In addition, house prices rose at their fastest pace in more than two years, up 2.6% over the period and 1.1% in October alone. Demand is now shifting from the lower quartile to the middle of the market, as interest rate cuts have increased borrowing capacity. The RBA took this data as a sign to hold its policy rate at 3.60%.

Portfolio activity

We introduced four new names to the portfolio over the quarter, in view of their margin resilience, capital discipline and structural growth, that would enhance the quality and return prospects of the Fund.

New holding Amcor (AMCR) is a global diversified packaging leader, which is repositioning its portfolio toward higher-growth segments like healthcare and beauty while integrating its recent acquisition, Berry Global, to unlock significant cost benefits by financial year 2028. Its planned sales of non-core assets should also help lift margins. Furthermore, its defensive cash flows support a dividend yield of more than 6%. Management’s track record in integration and cost discipline underpins our confidence in Amcor’s longer-term outlook.

We also invested in Metcash, a supplier of groceries, liquor and hardware to independent retailers, which offers cyclical upside from a housing recovery. Its growth prospects are underpinned by strong free cash flow, an attractive dividend yield and a reasonable valuation.

Another new position was in Medibank (OTCPK:MDBPF), the leading private health insurer in the country. It continues to outperform its domestic peers in a defensive industry, delivering industry-leading margins as its rivals struggle to survive. While policyholder growth has moderated from pandemic highs, its operational and retention improvements suggests that it can continue to stay ahead of the curve, while its deferred liability balance offers a cushion against claims catch-up, reinforcing margin stability.

Finally, Infratil (OTCPK:IFUUF) is a differentiated infrastructure play with a proven ability to crystallise value and redeploy capital into high-growth areas. Its portfolio’s tilt towards digital infrastructure, renewables and healthcare aligns with long-term structural growth themes. CDC, its Australian data centre asset, stands out, given that it is priced below its listed peer NextDC and yet arguably better positioned.

Conversely, we exited James Hardie to redirect capital towards better opportunities such as the initiations above.

Outlook

The RBA remains guided by data as it has been for some time now. The central bank’s rhetoric has turned noticeably more hawkish, and its latest forecasts mostly reflected the data surprises in the third quarter, with inflation, GDP and unemployment all revised higher. Rising inflation appears to be the central bank’s biggest concern for the moment.

Market valuations, meanwhile, have risen substantially, making it somewhat more challenging to find obvious value. In this context, the Fund is prudently taking profit where we find limited further upside catalysts and valuation support to manage risks. We will continue to take advantage of market dislocations to invest in high-quality companies, especially those poised to benefit as the tailwinds mentioned above begin to show tangible impact, while keeping a mindful eye on valuations. We remain partial to businesses with strong pricing power and defensive business moats, and we favor those with clear growth prospects that are aligned with long-term structural trends.

There are also risks associated with investing in Australia, including the risk of investing in a single-country Fund. Concentrating investments in the Australia region subjects the Fund to more volatility and greater risk of loss than geographically diverse funds.

The net asset value (NAV) is the value of an entity’s assets less the value of its liabilities. The market price is the current price at which an asset can be bought or sold. data.title: RFMZ: The Return Is Largely Return Of Capital
data.description:
How the machine works

A machine is composed of moving parts. What each of those parts does and how they interact determines - note, determines - the output of the machine. Thus, to grasp the output, we should take note of the individual moving parts.

Financial instruments are machines, and so the same is true of financial instruments. The internal moving parts interact in order to produce the output. Thus, to understand the output, we've got to look at those internal moving parts and their interactions.

RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income II (RFMZ)

This fund is a wholly laudable attempt to do something interesting in the municipal bond space. Sadly, it's something that seems not to really work. A pity because something interesting in muni bonds would be, well, interesting - it's not an investment space known for its excitement, after all.

The end result is a below-market income derived from the activities. Thus it's a no from me, and it should likely be a no from you.

This is not an absolute rejection, for of course it's always possible that a strategy could come good. But, well, there's been sufficient time, and it hasn't. Also, this isn't their only ride at this rodeo, and that one hasn't worked either. So, perhaps not.

Bond basics

When discussing a bond fund, I normally go through the basics of bonds themselves. As this particular fund is looking at a particular twist in these markets, I think it's better that those interested in those basics have a look at one of my other descriptions. Here for example. Start at "bond basics".

The essentials: we face both credit risk - not being repaid - and interest rate risk - changes in capital value from changes in background interest rates - when investing in bonds. Interest rate risk rises the longer the bond, but so also does the coupon being paid. This is just unfortunate; to gain a higher income, we have to take more interest rate risk. Credit risk we can minimise by diversification; with bonds, the usual way to do this is a bond fund.

Treasuries have interest rate risk and no credit risk, corporate bonds and munis both. However, munis pay lower coupons than either because the income is income tax free.

Higher-rate taxpayers looking for an income should definitely consider muni bond funds. For the full details of all of that, the explanations of how they interact, as I say, try this here.

What's RFMZ doing?

So, RFMZ is a muni bond fund, the income from it is income tax free and so on. In their direct portfolio, they go roughly intermediate - 10 year duration or so - and high credit rating:

From that we'd expect the income to be middling to low. High credit ratings mean lower coupons, intermediate tenor (ie, life of the bonds) lower than long term.

That income is listed as being pretty good, though:

Except, well, that's not really true. For as they also say:

Distributions may be paid from sources of income other than ordinary income, such as net realized short-term capital gains, net realized long-term capital gains and return of capital. The Fund estimates that 40.07% of the distribution is attributable to current year net investment income and 59.93% is attributable to return of capital.

So, 60% of that 7.9% is actually investors' own money being sent back to them as return of capital. What we might call "real" earnings are 3.16%. And by the standards of the muni market, that's not good. We can gain a full percentage point and a bit better in iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (SHYM) for example. Or two and a bit percentage points better in Nuveen Municipal High Income Opportunity Fund (NMZ). If we're investing for income, we should invest for income, right?

But that's not all RFMZ is doing

One of the reasons I've skimped a bit on the basic description of the bond markets here, referred it out, is to leave room - of course this is online, so there's plenty of space, but room in attention spans - to go through what this specific fund is trying to do so that we can understand what doesn't seem to be working. It's instructive, I think - could even inform our own investing in other areas of the muni bond market.

The trick here - no, sorry, the strategic insight - is that this is a closed-end fund. Our money does not flow into the fund when we buy, nor does it flow out when we sell. This means the fund can be leveraged, and most such CEFs are - to about 40%, as this one is. The insight is that the rollover of short-term borrowing by the fund costs less - there's less interest rate risk, as above - than the coupon of the long-term bonds held. This therefore juices the return to investors.

That our money does not flow out when we sell also means the fund cannot suffer a redemption cascade. This is akin to a bank run - a significant amount of selling would mean an open-ended fund must dump investments to provide the liquid cash to pay out. But a "really" significant amount of selling might mean taking losses, which then spooks other investors into another round of selling and thus the cascade. As with a bank run, no one wants to be the last in line for their money. This has actually happened to open-ended funds in illiquid investments too, notably in commercial real estate.

But there are trade-offs in everything. The one here is that a CEF can move well off its net asset value. Most do in fact, as this one does:

Premium/Discount 9.3%

Which is the insight that this fund is really running upon. Their own description:

While RiverNorth Capital Management, LLC (“RiverNorth”) believes markets are generally efficient, closed-end funds offer a distinct structure whereby investors can purchase a diversified fund and potentially generate additional return through the change in the relationship between the closed-end fund’s market price and Net Asset Value (NAV)

Or. as we might put it, buy CEFs trading at a discount and wait for that discount to close.

RiverNorth Tactical Municipal Closed-End Fund Strategy:

Seeks to generate returns through investments in investment companies primarily invested in municipal securities.

Seeks to derive value from the discount and premium spreads associated with closed-end funds

Typically invests in municipal closed end funds (CEFs), and exchange traded funds (ETFs)

There's the other portion of the fund that is run on those traditional lines of intermediate tenor and high credit rating munis directly.

OK, that's fine as a plan. Assuming that discounts to NAV close, then why not? It's interesting, surely worth a try and so on. But the big test is, well, does it work?

The answer seems to be no, it doesn't. Which is why we're getting this "true" return of distinctly less than market.

This also isn't a one shot proof. RiverNorth has another very similar fund:

They are not just investing in municipal bonds. They're also investing - that 33% of the strategy - in other closed end bond funds. We've said that a fund can wander off net asset value. So, why not invest in those that have done so and see if they return to closer to it? Or perhaps they will pay returns of capital to make themselves do so. Or, even, that higher yield might push up their market price?

That is, by investing in those well below net asset value we might be able to gain either a higher income - the yield to market price will be better than yield to NAV - or perhaps we can book some capital gains along the way.

Which is an appealing thought. One of those things definitely worth a try. And, as far as I can see, it doesn't work. The yield from RMI is 7.1% on NAV, 7.8% on market price. When we go on to note that's taxfree and compare with our tax e chart above that's actually well off that chart. So, this is working really well, right?

And when we correct that for return of capital:

This means we should probably take that 7.8% and times it by 0.4 to give the actual earned distribution here, with the rest of it just being our own capital being returned to us. So, we're really getting 3.12% here. Which is not good, even by the standards of muni funds.

So, we end up in that other, similar strategy fund, about where we are with this one.

This is an interesting strategy. It is true that many closed end funds trade below net asset value. Being able to capture their market price's return to asset value would seem to be a useful strategy.

But that's in theory - what we actually want to know is whether it works in practice. And my analysis here is that it doesn't, in fact, work. In the sense that the return achieved is actually pretty low. 3.12% is low by the standards of muni bond funds, once we've adjusted for that return of capital.

So, no, this does not work for me. Better is available elsewhere. We should file this as "nice idea, doesn't quite work" I think.

Well, yes, that is about it.

Why I'm wrong.

It is always possible for a strategy to come right after some period of it not doing so. So that could bite me at the ankles; of course it could.

It's also possible that some investors would prefer to have an income with also the possibility of an extra gain and are willing to have a lower income while they wait to find out.

But those are both rather different from does this strategy work now?

My opinion

I'm wholly fine with the idea of the strategy. An idea that needs to be tested. Investing in closed-end bond funds to benefit from their reversion to net asset value from a substantial discount.

But once tested - or twice, in fact - it doesn't seem to work. There's no "excess" income on offer here; in fact, the return is distinctly sub-market. So, this fund, RFMZ, and its brother, RMI, don't work for me.

I think the much more interesting point here is the more general lesson. Funds trading at a discount to NAV don't, necessarily, return to NAV. If they did, then RFMZ and RMI would be paying well above market distributions. Now that is an interesting finding for our own investments in all sorts of funds, no? That reversion to NAV is not to be assumed?

data.title: EchoStar: Fair For Its Operations, SpaceX For Free
data.description:
EchoStar Corporation (SATS) has had a big run this year, and the stock has been surprisingly flat the last couple of months, in spite of the general nervousness in the market. One reason is that people feel more confident in its connection to SpaceX, a sentiment that I share and that informs my Buy thesis today.

Financial History

To understand the current opportunity, one needs to look at the changes EchoStar has experienced over time.

First is the very small revenue prior to the 2020s, followed by a sharp jump in reported revenue in 2021. Even though it's declined substantially, reported revenue has remained much higher than the earlier period. This wasn't exactly a result of growth for EchoStar. Rather, 2021 marked a period in which they gained a majority stake in DISH, which culminated in a full merger in 2023. This expanded the business to include DISH's TV and wireless businesses.

While this resulted in higher free cash flow for the SATS ticker, we also see that the merger has come with additional cash burn.

Most of this is due to additions to property & equipment exceeding the operating cash flows. In 2024, OCF was $1,253M, while these additions were $1,545M. In addition to that, there are outflows from capitalized interest related to the regulatory authorizations. In their SEC filings, EchoStar actually defines their free cash flow as OCF minus these two expenses.

For more context, this is an expense related to spectrum licenses for the business, which are very much necessary. Taking that into account, 2022's FCF was actually about $1B less, turning it negative. It required EchoStar to take on more debt to raise the necessary capital.

In spite of this, the stock is up about 190% over the past year, with the biggest jump occurring in late August. This followed a deal with AT&T (T) to purchase spectrum licenses from them for $23B. SpaceX soon offered a similar deal for $17B (half cash, half SpaceX stock).

With a look at the third quarter's balance sheet, the value to SATS is clear here. Most assets are these expensive licenses and unlikely to cover their $21.8B of long-term debt in a pinch. With this deal, they will have the cash on hand to reduce some of it and cover the rest.

So the SATS story has been a rapidly evolving one in the last few years. The deal with AT&T gives them a chance to move in a new direction.

Current Operations

EchoStar's evolution is ongoing, but there's a newer business ahead at this point. It still consists of the following operations:

  • Pay TV
  • Wireless: Hybrid Mobile Network Operator ("MNO")
  • Broadband and Satellite Services ("BSS")

The biggest contributor to revenue currently is Pay TV, which consists of their Dish and Sling TV streaming services.

Not only is it the largest contributor to revenue, but it was also the only major contributor to profitability. BSS is a much smaller business, and Wireless has been very capital-intensive. The deals with AT&T and SpaceX, however, have transformed Wireless into what management calls a "hybrid MNO."

The deals therefore not only allow EchoStar to reduce its leverage; the utilization of AT&T's infrastructure for Boost Mobile (Wireless subsidiary) means that it will be capital-light going forward, which should result in less overhead for this segment. The SpaceX deal will connect their mobile service to the satellite network, giving customers a dead-zone free product of connectivity from terrestrial and orbital coverage. Wireless now comes down to maintaining the cloud service that connects to AT&T and SpaceX.

Outlook and Valuation

The near-term impact on revenue from all of this is not clear, particularly as the deals are not final yet, requiring regulatory approval. Nevertheless, the deals are a result of EchoStar's efforts to satisfy the FCC's pushback on its previous strategy, so I'm inclined to think they will ultimately be approved by early 2026.

At a recent price of $73 per share, SATS carries a market cap near $21B. It's not clear to me that they will enjoy much growth in TV, mobile, or broadband, and so I don't think paying a big multiple for SATS makes a lot of sense.

I think it's reasonable to guess that the cost improvements, post-transaction, should get FCF back into the $2B range, particularly as the capitalized interest on their licenses will disappear, along with hundreds of millions in interest expense as they retire large portions of their debt. This would make the current market cap only a multiple of about 10, which I think is reasonable.

Again, this is only counting the current business. There's also the $8.5B equity stake in SpaceX that they are to receive from the deal, which is essentially "free value" for what is fair on the operating business, and it depends on the upside of SpaceX over time to see how that accrues to SATS.

Risks

There are a few risks here. The obvious one is that the government about-faces and doesn't approve these deals. This would put EchoStar in a precarious financial position. Even if this occurs, it exposes the company to risks related to AT&T. It's worth remembering that they faced their own debt hurdles a few years ago, forcing them to cut their dividend and restructure. Kicking the capital requirements to AT&T does not mean they are gone, and a weak AT&T could hurt EchoStar's revenue.

Similarly, as SpaceX isn't a public company with widely available financial data, its upside is tough to value precisely. Outgoing CEO Hamid Akhavan explained during the Q3 earnings call:

But first, we want to mention that we are not insiders to SpaceX. So we have no inside knowledge of SpaceX. And Charlie and I's views are 100% aligned and common on how great a SpaceX is, but that's just personal views.

This was during Q&A, following a question about what's considered a good valuation for SpaceX. It is possible that SpaceX's upside may not be as grand as hoped if its growth potential is already priced or not sufficiently realized over time. A year ago, a tender offer valued the company at $350B, which is a large valuation as it is, so it's conceivable that returns through SATS may be small for a while.

Conclusion

While SATS still has some hurdles to clear and some uncertainties ahead, I think the current price hasn't captured the full upside from the pending SpaceX stake, and so I rate it a Buy. With the information we currently have, I think it gets fair as the market cap approaches $30B, and an entry price under $90 provides some safety until the deals close. At that point, reassessment and a revaluation will be in order.
